conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have a child - my son Alex. He's 17 and lives with me here in England. I wanted to check if I can claim Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and decided not to continue with A-levels or college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to understand your situation fully.

Are you (or your partner, if you have one) responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does he live with you and do you support him financially?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives with me at home. As for supporting him financially, well, he actually has a full-time job now - he works at a local warehouse, so he's earning his own money.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to confirm - is Alex 17 years old currently, or has he already turned 18?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: Child Benefit can be claimed for children under 16, or for young people under 20 if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex is 17 and is not currently in full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels or college) or approved training, he doesn't meet the eligibility criteria.

The fact that he's working full-time at a warehouse confirms that he's not in the required education or training programme. Once children over 16 leave education or training and enter full-time employment, Child Benefit entitlement ends.

If Alex were to return to approved education or training in the future (and is still under 20), you could potentially claim Child Benefit again at that point.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and lives with the claimant in England. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can claim Child Benefit for children under 16, OR for children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 17 and is NOT in full-time non-advanced education or approved training (he finished school and did not continue with A-levels or college, and now works full-time at a warehouse), he does not meet the criteria for Child Benefit. The fact that he has a full-time job further confirms he is not in the required education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. While Alex is under 20 and lives with the claimant, he does not meet the eligibility criteria because he is not in approved education or training. Child Benefit can only be claimed for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, which Alex is not. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
